coredata.dc:description: Widespread environmental pollution from heavy metals poses severe health hazards, necessitating advanced detection methods. This study presents an innovative approach utilizing Mesua Ferrea Carbon Dots (MFCDs) incorporated biopolymeric nanocomposite films for optical identification of heavy metal ions (Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+). A blend of Na-Alginate and Agarose biopolymer was prepared as a matrix. Hydrothermally synthesized MFCDs possess negative zeta potential with their characteristic photoluminescence property. The MFCDs incorporated Na-Alginate-Agarose biopolymeric nanocomposite (MAA) film displayed enhanced hydrophilicity and UV absorption behaviour, showcasing its potential for optical detection of heavy metal ions. The optical analysis like fluorescence intensity and photoluminescence lifetime studies of the MAA film in response to heavy metal ions were thoroughly studied. The interaction dynamics highlighted specific metal ion quenching capabilities, emphasizing the potential of MFCDs coated film for sensitive heavy metal detection. The quenching mechanism for Fe3+ ions underscored the lowest level of detection in comparison with Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of the optical detection phenomenon. Our research work provides a robust and scientifically validated solid platform for development of economically viable and environmentally benign optical sensor as an alternative of other existing methods for detection of heavy metal ions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Widespread environmental pollution from heavy metals poses severe health hazards, necessitating advanced detection methods. This study presents an innovative approach utilizing Mesua Ferrea Carbon Dots (MFCDs) incorporated biopolymeric nanocomposite films for optical identification of heavy metal ions (Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+). A blend of Na-Alginate and Agarose biopolymer was prepared as a matrix. Hydrothermally synthesized MFCDs possess negative zeta potential with their characteristic photoluminescence property. The MFCDs incorporated Na-Alginate-Agarose biopolymeric nanocomposite (MAA) film displayed enhanced hydrophilicity and UV absorption behaviour, showcasing its potential for optical detection of heavy metal ions. The optical analysis like fluorescence intensity and photoluminescence lifetime studies of the MAA film in response to heavy metal ions were thoroughly studied. The interaction dynamics highlighted specific metal ion quenching capabilities, emphasizing the potential of MFCDs coated film for sensitive heavy metal detection. The quenching mechanism for Fe3+ ions underscored the lowest level of detection in comparison with Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions, contributing to a comprehensive understanding of the optical detection phenomenon. Our research work provides a robust and scientifically validated solid platform for development of economically viable and environmentally benign optical sensor as an alternative of other existing methods for detection of heavy metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The fundamental prerequisite for a sustainable environment is to preserve the integrity of a clean and healthy atmosphere. The widespread environmental pollution stands as a significant drawback of the current era of industrialization, primarily attributed to the anthropogenic discharge of domestic as well as industrial wastewater. Within this spectrum of pollutants, heavy metals emerge as pivotal ecological health threat, securing a premier spot among the toxicity index [1]. Having an elevated density (five times greater than water), heavy metals exhibit a toxic and bio-accumulative nature [2]. Heavy metal ions gravely impact on both individual wellness and the natural ecology due to its non-biodegradable nature and persist throughout the food chain, subjecting living being to extremely high toxicity levels. Cu (II), Hg(II), Pb(II), Cd(II) and Fe(III) are some of the commonly detected heavy metals in ground water, household wastewater as well as industrial effluents which can cause significant health and environmental hazards. The US EPA (Environmental Protection Agency) and WHO (World Health Organization) put limits on the allowable heavy metal ion concentrations in potable water and wastewater effluents while keeping sustainability into consideration. In drinking water, WHO specifies that the permissible values of Hg (II), Cd (II), and Pb (II) are 0.006 ppm, 0.005 ppm, and 0.01 ppm, respectively and these data changes to 0.001 ppm, 0.003 ppm, and 0.01 ppm in wastewater effluents [3]. The US EPA likewise reported these figures for Hg (II), Cd (II), and Pb (II) in drinking water to be 0.002 ppm, 0.005 ppm, and 0.05 ppm respectively [4]. While copper is an essential trace element but due to its excessive exposure, the global aquatic environment is experiencing a spike in Cu (II) pollution can cause neurological problems, gastrointestinal issues etc. Its prolonged exposure can cause Wilson's disease resulting in liver and brain damage. Thus, drinking water and wastewater effluents should have a maximum concentration of not >1.3 ppm and 5 ppm of Cu (II), respectively, according to the estimation from WHO and US EPA [5]. Undesirable presence of Fe (III) in water may cause harsh, metallic taste and oxidized precipitation. WHO in 1993, sets an acceptable limit of 0.3 ppm to 1 ppm for iron in drinking water [6]. Excessive accumulation of iron in tissues can adversely affect skeletal muscle function that diminishes muscle contractility and disrupts its endocrine functions leading neurodegenerative disorders. Again, lead (Pb) toxicity has been associated with hypertension, neurological disorders, cognitive impairments, etc. Mercury (Hg), one of the heavy metals, exists in the organic, inorganic, and elemental forms, which usually finds in human through consumption of contaminated foods and water or via inhalation of air. Exposure to Hg(II), is associated with severe health issues in children, including deafness, mental retardation, cerebral palsy, and blindness. According to WHO, exposure to elemental Hg can lead to a range of symptoms such as fever, cough, tremors, malaise, axonal sensor motor polyneuropathy, gingivitis, hallucinations, mercurial erythrism etc. Even low doses of Hg can adversely affect the neuronal systems of a growing foetus. Cadmium, a non-essential element for human body, can exhibit toxic effects even at low concentrations. It is also released through tobacco and rated as carcinogen for human health. It accumulates in the human lungs, bones, kidneys, liver, nerve tissues, leading to their damage as well as malfunction. To address and prevent the adverse properties and effects linked to heavy metals, there is a pressing need for development of sensing technologies for detection of trace heavy metals in water resources [7].

Detection of heavy metals involves employing a range of conventional analytical methods like Atomic Emission Spectroscopy (AEM), microprobes, capillary electrophoresis (CE), X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy (XFS), Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP), Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS), High performance Liquid chromatography (HPLC) etc. Despite the ability of these techniques to identify multiple heavy metal ions at bare minimum concentrations, they have the limitations like high expenses, technical complications and lengthy processing times. Moreover, these techniques are not portable and cannot function as on-site devices. This underscores the growing demand for an accessible, cost-effective, and sensitive device capable of on-spot observation. Biochemical sensors utilizing fluorescence evaluation offer a promising solution, addressing challenges related to accessibility, point-of-care efficiency, minimal detectable range, sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy providing both quantitative and qualitative particulars. Fluorescence-based optical evaluation systems serve the dual purpose of point-of-care capability and transportability, often leveraging cost-effective optical fibres as superior alternatives to non-portable sensing techniques [8]. These systems, combined with fluorescent sensing measures, utilize various fluorophores, including quantum dots, fluorescent dyes, organic-metal frameworks (OMF), and probes, for detecting analyte across diverse matrices. They have been extensively studied for environmental monitoring, detecting various analytes [9–12].

Carbon dots (CDs) represent as zero-dimensional nanomaterial, characterized by intriguing features such as non-toxicity, biological viability and tunability. In comparison to organic fluorophores like some of organic dyes, CDs offer distinct advantages, including excitation across a wide range of wavelengths, broad excitation-narrow emission characteristics, prolonged photoluminescence duration, excellent photostability, along with reduced photobleaching [13]. Significantly, CDs outperform inorganic quantum dots such as CdTe, Pb-based QDs and CdSe, addressing concerns related to discharge and ecological toxicity. The distinctive features of CDs position them as favourable indicators for detecting various analytes, including dyes, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and heavy metals, particularly in health and ecological investigations where toxicity stands as a paramount concern [14].

Keeping concern for environment sustainability, use of biogenic resources for sensing purposes has been extensively explored [15,16]. In our present study, we aimed to develop a novel fluorescent carbon dot from Mesua Ferrea seeds, mostly found in some of the South Asian countries, comprise a variety of bioactive components with significant nutritive and therapeutic value including proteins, lipids, carbohydrates, saturated and unsaturated fatty acids. When undertaking quantitative biochemical evaluation, Mesua ferrea seeds were found to contain total protein content of ∼7.00–7.19 g % [17] which may be responsible for the nitrogen content in it. Thus, the different compounds present in the seed are solely accountable for the essential functional groups present in the zero-dimensional nanomaterial CD synthesized from it.

A solid portable optical sensing platform, represented as MAA, is conceptualized to address the inherent drawbacks of existing methods by combining a biopolymeric film, comprising sodium alginate and agarose, with Mesua ferrea-derived CDs (MFCDs). The -COO−, -OH groups in sodium alginate provide multiple binding sites for bivalent and trivalent metal ions through electrostatic forces of attraction and also with hydrogen bonding. When modified with agarose biopolymer, the strength of sodium alginate film can be enhanced. MFCDs incorporated Alginate-Agarose (MAA) film has several advantages over other reported traditional sensing platforms. MAA film exhibits simple preparation technique having enhanced sensitivity and selectivity due to the unique characteristics of MFCDs, allowing it for the detection of very low concentrations of targeted analytes. The interaction of the composite film with metal ions in aqueous solutions can be facilitated by its excellent hydrophilicity. Additionally, the biocompatibility and biodegradability of alginate and agarose biopolymers make MAA film environmentally benign over synthetic polymers, addressing the concerns over the pollution occur through plastic wastes. MFCD integration into Alginate-Agarose film would enhance the mechanical strength and stability of MAA film thereby ensuring reliable performance towards specific heavy metal sensing with consuming less time. Overall, these features would make MAA film as a sustainable sensing platform towards heavy metal ions.

This detailed examination underscores the innovative and distinctive contributions of our approach to the advancement of sensing technologies for trace heavy metal ions, presenting a robust and scientifically validated framework for future research and environmental monitoring. Table 1 highlights the advantages of various methods including biopolymeric thin film based optical sensing method for heavy metal ion detection and also the schematic representation of our research work is given in Scheme 1 .

Copper sulfate and cadmium chloride were procured from Merck Pvt. Ltd. (India), quinine sulphate dihydrate, glycerol and mercuric chloride were purchased from HIMEDIA, lead nitrate and sodium alginate were purchased from SRL, ferric chloride and sulfuric acid from MOLYCHEM, while agarose was acquired from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. Whatman filter paper with 2.5 μm pore diameter was consistently utilized throughout the experiments. All chemicals were of analytical (AR) grade and employed without additional purification. Locally available Mesua Ferrea seeds were used for the synthesis of carbon dots. Milli-Q ultrapure water (>18 MΩ cm) was the chosen solvent throughout the experimental work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Mesua Ferrea carbon dot (MFCD)
ce:section.ce:para: A simple hydrothermal technique [25] was used for the synthesis of carbon dots (CD) derived from Mesua ferrea seeds. The process initiated with the crushing of raw seeds, followed by a seven-day oven drying period at 50 °C to ensure optimal conditions. Subsequently, 1.6 g of the dried seed mass were placed into a 100 ml teflon coated stainless steel autoclave chamber, maintaining a seed mass to deionized water ratio of 1:5 (W/V). The autoclave chamber was then heated to 170 °C in a muffle furnace, undergoing a four-hour reaction. Allowing the mixture to reach room temperature, the solution was filtered using Whatman filter paper with a 2.5 μm pore size. Following an hour of probe sonication, the filtrate was centrifuged for 10-min at 12,000 rpm. After careful removal, the supernatant was diluted 2× times. This stock solution exhibited distinctive bright green luminescent light emission at 365 nm under UV light, which confirmed the synthesis of MFCDs. The resultant solution was securely stored at 4 °C for subsequent characterization and utilization in further experiments. Fig. 1(a) represents the processes involved in the preparation of MFCDs.

After that, the yield of the dissolved carbon dot was determined gravimetrically using the solution directly obtained after hydrothermal treatment followed by filtration and sonication (without dilution) and it was found to be 0.3 mg/ml. Further investigations revealed that reducing the seed mass while maintaining the same water amount resulted in a decreased amount of dissolved carbon dots and no increase in the carbon dot yield could be achieved by increasing the amount of Mesua Ferrea seed mass as confirmed by gravimetric analysis. The volume of dissolved carbon dots reached a saturation point at the specified seed mass to water ratio of 1:5 (W/V). This suggests that maintaining this ratio is essential for maximizing yield and ensuring effective synthesis of MFCDs. Again, the temperature and time of the reaction was optimized as minimum required to get the highest yield to design it as an energy efficient process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MFCD coated Na-alginate/ agarose film
ce:section.ce:para: MFCDs/ Na-alginate/agarose nanocomposite film was intricately fabricated using the solution casting technique [23]. The process commenced with the formulation of a boiling solution, consisting of 40 ml of the stock solution containing Mesua Ferrea-derived carbon dots (MFCDs). To enhance the film's flexibility and structural integrity, 60 μl of glycerol was incorporated as a plasticizer. The subsequent steps involved the incremental addition of 0.2 g of agarose powder into the solution, followed by microwave heating for 60 s. This facilitated the dissolution of agarose, ensuring its homogeneous integration into the solution. Next, 0.2 g of sodium alginate powder was introduced, and the solution underwent an additional 60 s of microwave treatment. This meticulous procedure ensured a consistent and thorough mixing of the components, crucial for the subsequent properties of the nanocomposite film. The homogeneous solution was then delicately cast onto a petri-plate, and the resulting composite was subjected to a controlled drying process in an oven for 24 h at a temperature of 50 °C. This extended drying period allowed for the evaporation of residual solvent, leading to the formation of a nanocomposite film with a targeted thickness of approximately 0.035 mm. The film, designated as MAA was carefully detached from the petri plates and stored at room temperature (∼30 °C) within an air-tight zip bag. Fig. 1(b) illustrates the schematic diagram outlining the sequential steps involved in the preparation of the MFCDs incorporated Na-alginate/agarose film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical sensing study using MFCDs coated Na-alginate/ agarose film
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared film was segmented into pieces with dimensions of 1 cm × 2 cm. To assess the film's response to varying concentrations of heavy metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+), solutions were prepared with different concentrations (5 ppm, 1 ppm, 0.5 ppm, 0.1 ppm, 0.05 ppm, 0.01 ppm and 0.005 ppm). Each film segment was submersed for 5 min in individual heavy metal solutions of 10 ml and qualitatively analysed for fluorescence intensity under a UV inspection cabinet. This step provided a visual indication of the pronounced quenching activity observed across different metal ion concentrations, elucidating the film's responsiveness to specific heavy metal ions. Quantitative analysis was done by UV analysis for a comprehensive examination of the sensitive absorbance characteristics exhibited by the treated films. This analytical approach allowed for the precise measurement of notable variations in the absorbance spectra across the wavelength range 220–900 nm. The identified variations were attributed to the distinctive concentrations of different metal ions, providing a detailed and quantifiable insight into the film's sensitivity of heavy metal contaminants.

Perkin Elmer 2000 spectrophotometer were used for Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) analysis of carbon dots and the fabricated films. The morphological study was done by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) with the WITec alpha300RAS. The surface overview characterization was done through Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM), JEOL TEM-2100 Plus model. For elemental percentage determination and binding energy shift analysis, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was employed with monochromatic radiation of Al Kα at a potential of 1486.6 eV. The Sur PASSTM Electrokinetic Analyzer determined the flowing potential and surface charge accumulation of the fabricated films. Thermal stability of the films was evaluated through the PERKIN Elmer PC series, DSC 7. Contact angle measurements of water on the fabricated films were carried out at normal room temperature using a standard contact angle analyzer (DM-501, Kyowa Interface Science Co. Ltd.). Fluorescence related all evaluations of CDs and fabricated films were performed with a Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (FS) HORIBA Instruments: FLUOROLOG-3. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was obtained utilizing a Shimadzu UV-2600i spectrophotometer. Tensile strength analysis involved the “Tinius Olsen 5ST” model with a loaded capacity of 2.5 kN, following desiccation of a 10 cm × 2.5 cm film overnight. RAMAN spectra of MFCDs were obtained by using DXR2 SmartRaman instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing the water absorbency and solubility % of MAA film
ce:section.ce:para:
The dry film fragment was cut into a dimension of “2 cm X 2 cm” and the weight found to be 0.03 g. This piece of dry sample was kept in a beaker containing distilled water for around 20 min until the biopolymeric film reached its swollen equilibrium. The film was weighed in time to time and the water absorbency calculated by using the following formula: [26].

The water solubility of MAA film was assessed by a previously reported method with some modifications [27,28]. Initially, the sample films were dried at 50 °C until a constant weight, cut into 2 cm × 2 cm dimensions and weighed accurately (W0). The strips were subsequently immersed in 10 ml of distilled water for a duration of 24 h. Then the samples were removed, and excess surface water was blotted off using filter paper. The films were then dried again in an oven at 50 °C and reweighed (Wt). The solubility percentage of the films in water was calculated using Eq. (2).

where, W0 is the initial weight of MAA film and Wt is the weight after time t.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum Yield (QY) estimation of Mesua Ferrea Carbon Dot (MFCD)
ce:section.ce:para: The Quantum Yield of Mesua Ferrea Carbon Dot (MFCD) was determined by using slope method (Fig. S1). For this purpose, quinine sulfate solution was prepared in 0.1 N H2SO4 as reference (Quantum Yield = 0.546). The absorbance of MFCD and quinine sulfate was determined by UV visible Spectrophotometer and plotted against integrated fluorescence intensity (λex = 360 nm) obtained from Fluorescence spectrophotometric analysis. The detailed method of calculation of QY is mentioned in supplementary information.

Due to the low biodegradability, heavy metal ion poses significant hazards to both the environment and human health. Availability of conventional techniques to detect heavy metal ions are costly and hence there is a need of simple and cost-effective method to detect heavy metal ions. The fabricated novel Mesua ferrea derived fluorescent CDs incorporated alginate and agarose composite (MAA) hydrogel film is a simple, non-toxic and sufficient water stable portable device to detect specific heavy metal ions present in wastewater sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the extracted carbon dot
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthesis of MFCDs, natural Mesua Ferrea seeds were utilized as a novel precursor. The detailed synthetic procedure was explained in the experimental Section 2.2. From the High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis of MFCDs in Fig. 2(a), it was clearly revealed the nanoparticle formation from Mesua Ferrea seeds dispersed in deionized water with a size <10 nm and a size distribution graph obtained from the TEM images has included in the supplementary section (Fig. S2). The result was further supported by DLS analysis, as shown in Fig. 2(b), which also indicated the presence of nanoparticles in the MFCD solution having diameters <10 nm. Furthermore, the negative zeta potential value of −18 mV observed for the synthesized MFCD indicates a significant negative charge on the surface as shown in Fig. 2(c). This information is essential for understanding the colloidal stability and interactions of MFCDs in various applications, as the zeta potential reflects the electrostatic repulsion between particles, influencing their dispersion and aggregation behaviour in solution. Also, the negative zeta potential value of MFCDs have significant role in detection of metal ions as it may have electrostatic interaction with the metal ions.

MFCDs exhibit remarkable photoluminescence characteristics, as evidenced by their emission spectra in response to various excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 2(d). When subjected to excitation at wavelengths of 380, 390, 400, 410, 420, 430, and 440 nm, the emitted fluorescence displayed a distinctive red shift in the wavelength 440, 445, 450, 454, 478, 490 and 496 nm respectively. This phenomenon is indicative of the quantum confinement effect, where the emission wavelength becomes more extended as the wavelength of excitation rises [29]. The red shift in emission is a characteristic feature of nanoscale carbon dots and underscores the unique optical properties of MFCDs, making them suitable for diverse applications in sensing, imaging, and optoelectronic devices. This observation provides valuable insights into the photophysical behaviour of MFCDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of MFCDs were analysed to understand their optical properties and electronic transitions. The spectra revealed a prominent peak in the range 200–380 nm, indicating the occurrence of specific electronic transitions within the carbon dot structure. This absorption peak can be correlated to the n-π* transition of carbonyl (C=O) groups and the π-π* transition of conjugated carbon‑carbon (C=C) bonds. The n-π* transition involves the excitation of electrons from non-bonding electron pairs (n) to the antibonding π* orbital, typically associated with carbonyl groups. Simultaneously, the π-π* transition involves the excitation of electrons within the π-bonding system of conjugated carbon atoms. The observed absorption peak at around 250–270 nm signifies the energy required for these electronic transitions, providing valuable insights into the molecular structure and bonding characteristics of MFCD. With a maximum absorption in the specific range and an extended tail that continues towards the visible wavelengths, the carbon dots' optical absorption peak was found in the UV region is shown in Fig. 2(e). This information is crucial for understanding the optical behaviour of carbon dots [30,31].

As shown in Fig. 2(f), FTIR spectrum revealed the distinctive broad peak at 3436.69 cm−1 for stretching vibrations of OH and NH functional groups, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino moieties. Stretching vibrations at 2925.17 cm−1 further confirmed the presence of CH functional group. Peaks associated with CO stretching (1618.89 cm−1) and C-O-C stretching (1056.78 cm−1) vibrations provided evidence of carbonyl groups and ether linkages in the carbon dot matrix. Bending vibrations of CH and NH at 1384.90 cm−1 added to the functional groups identified. Additionally, the distinctive peaks at 672 cm−1 and 877.14 cm−1 corresponding to CC bending and CH bending vibrations elucidated the unique structural features of the carbon dots [30–32]. The comprehensive FTIR analysis collectively indicates the presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups on the carbon dot surface.

The surface elemental analysis of MFCDs was performed using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS), providing valuable insights towards the elemental composition along with the chemical states of the synthesized carbon dots. In the full-scan spectrum, distinct peaks corresponding to carbon (C 1s, 284.98 eV), nitrogen (N 1s, 400.94 eV), and oxygen (O 1s, 531.5 eV) were detected, confirming the presence of these elements in the carbon dot structure. Further detailed examination of the C 1s spectra revealed specific peaks at 284.98 eV and 288.1 eV. The peak at 284.98 eV can be attributed to C-C/C=C bonds, signifying the presence of aliphatic carbon in the carbon dots. The peak at 288.1 eV suggests the presence of CN or CO bonds on the surface of the carbon dots, highlighting the existence of nitrogen-containing functional groups [33,34]. This comprehensive XPS analysis provides elemental and chemical state information, confirming the diverse composition of MFCDs as shown in Fig. 2(g), (h), (i) and (j). The elemental constituents of MFCDs were further assured by using Energy Dispersive X-Ray analysis or in short EDX, which showed 58.79 % carbon content and 41.21 % oxygen content (Fig. S3 in supporting information).

The Raman spectra of synthesized MFCDs reveals two significant bands, one at ∼1383 cm−1 and another at ∼1587 cm−1 in the supplementary information (Fig. S4). These corresponds to the D band associated with disordered sp3-hybridized carbon and the G band associated with ordered sp2-hybridized carbon in a graphitic structure [35]. The intensities of these bands are denoted as ID and IG, respectively, and their values were extracted from the spectra. An intensity ratio of I
D /I
G <1 suggests that the synthesized carbon materials are well graphitized. In this case, the measured ratio for the MFCDs was ∼0.88, indicating a relatively low level of defects in their carbon structure. This finding underscores the quality and structural integrity of the synthesized MFCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and characterization of the fabricated MAA film
ce:section.ce:para: To design the intended biopolymeric film, a biobased polymer sodium alginate was chosen because of its effective film forming capabilities which makes them valuable for implication in various applications. The -COO− and -OH group present in sodium alginate moiety aids to strong interaction (metal ions are generally used for crosslinking of alginate biopolymer) with the divalent and trivalent metal ions, facilitates for the absorption of the various compounds within its structure [29,36]. To utilize this affinity of alginate towards metal ions we have converted this biopolymer into its film form by solution casting method. However, the bare alginate films found to have very poor water stability. Hence to improve the stability of alginate films in aqueous medium for further applications we have blended this biopolymer with another biopolymeric material i.e. agarose. The physicochemical properties of the prepared sodium alginate and agarose composite film was modulated by incorporating novel MFCDs which improves the hydrophilicity, mechanical strength and surface topography as well. Glycerol was added as plasticizer to enhance the flexibility of the composite film. The intermolecular hydrogen interactions between the hydroxyl groups of the composite polymeric matrix with the functional groups present in MFCDs creating a selective functional composite film for the specific sensing applications.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was employed to investigate the surface texture or morphology of both agarose-sodium alginate (bare) film and the MFCD-decorated agarose‑sodium alginate nanocomposite (MAA) film. The SEM images in Fig. 3(a) and 3(b) reveal a distinctive and consistent arrangement of carbon dots on the nanocomposite film, showcasing the homogeneous distribution of MFCDs onto the agarose‑sodium alginate matrix. This comparative analysis underscores the observable difference in the surface structure between the bare film and the nanocomposite film, confirming the successful decoration of carbon dots and their uniform distribution on the nanocomposite film. Again, atomic force microscopy (AFM) technique was employed to analyse the surface topography of MFCD-coated alginate-agarose composite film. The analysis of surface roughness of a biopolymeric film plays a significant role in sensing applications. In Fig. 3(c) and 3(d), the AFM image of bare film shows roughness average (Ra) of 26.93 and that of MFCDs coated MAA film shows 53.42. The insertion of the MFCDs to the bare film was responsible for enhancing surface roughness of the bare biopolymeric film [37]. Increasing film's surface roughness enhances the surface functionality, and has greater detection capability.

The detailed surface functional groups present in the fabricated MAA film was analysed via FTIR spectroscopy. A broad and prominent peak at approximately 3450 cm−1 suggests the existence of O-H/N-H stretching vibration absorption bands [38]. Further characterization reveals specific features associated with different functional groups: the stretching vibrations of CH2 groups at 2927.28 cm−1 and the CO group at 1630 cm−1 [31,39], indicating the existence of carboxylic groups. The olefinic configuration in the sp2 –CH group is identified by the peak at 1428.84 cm−1, while the stretching of –C–O is denoted by the peak at 1122.56 cm−1 [40]. Additionally, distinctive peaks include CC bending at approximately 934 cm−1 and CC stretching at 2165 cm−1. Peaks at 1384 cm−1 and 1049 cm−1 are attributed to CH and NH bending vibrations, and CN stretching, respectively. This comprehensive analysis underscores the intricate composition of the fabricated film, detailing the various functional groups present on its surface. Fig. S5 represents the FTIR spectra of Bare and MAA film in supporting information.

Furthermore, XPS survey spectra demonstrate that three main peaks of C, N, and O are combined, and their amplification adequately characterized their presence in the fabricated MAA film. The peaks obtained in the deconvoluted high resolution C 1s spectra at 284.8 eV, 286.3 eV, and 288 eV correspond to C-C/C=C, CO, and C=N/C=O, respectively. Meanwhile, the deconvoluted O 1s spectra, revealed peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.81 eV that are accordingly ascribed to CO and CO. Furthermore, the presence of NH bonds is indicated by a peak in the N 1s spectra at about 400 eV [40]. Fig. 4(a), (b), (c) and (d) represents the deconvoluted XPS spectra of MAA film along with its survey spectra.

An analysis was carried out to study the stress-strain relationship by comparing bare (sodium alginate-agarose) film with MAA film. The bare film's tensile stress and elongation at break were determined to be ∼ 32 MPa and ∼ 2.7 %, while that of MAA film's values were ∼ 39 MPa and ∼ 2.69 % (Fig. 4(e)) correspondingly. Due to the intermolecular interactions between the functional groups of the MFCDs moiety and the biopolymeric matrix, the incorporation of MFCDs enhanced the tensile strength of the alginate-agarose biopolymeric matrix by ∼ 22 %. The film's elongation at break or tensile strain was remain almost unaffected by the insertion of green carbon dots which eventually increased intermolecular interaction but surface flexibility of MAA film remained significantly the same [41]. Again, both the films were subjected to turbulence in a mechanical shaker for about an hour, and the resulting stress-strain relationships were evaluated (Fig. S6). The exposure to turbulent conditions may disrupt the internal bonding within the films, potentially leading to a reduction in their stress values. The measured stress values were approximately ∼ 17.4 MPa for bare film and ∼ 21 MPa for MAA biopolymeric film. These findings indicate that while turbulence can adversely affect the mechanical strength of the films, the incorporation of MFCD provides enhancement in stress resistance capacity compared to the bare film. Moreover, turbulence produced in a mechanical shaker can induce molecular displacements within the biopolymeric matrix, while the subsequent absorption of moisture may reduce the films' stiffness, leading to an increased elongation at break.

The UV absorption characteristics of both the bare film and MFCD decorated agarose‑sodium alginate (MAA) nanocomposite film were systematically investigated and shown in Fig. 4(f). In the UV absorption spectrum of the bare film, a prominent absorption peak was discerned at 271.3 nm. Intriguingly, the UV absorption profile of the MAA film exhibited a noticeable bathochromic shift, with the absorption intensity peak at 321.2 nm. This shift in absorption characteristics is attributed to the incorporation of MFCDs within the nanocomposite film [29,42].

The observed bathochromic shift implies alterations in the electronic structure of the film due to the introduction of MFCD. The distinct conjugate aromatic structures inherent to MFCD are known to facilitate π → π* transitions, contributing to the modified UV absorption properties of the MAA film. The enhanced absorption intensity at a longer wavelength signifies the interaction and integration of MFCD, influencing the optical behaviour of the nanocomposite film. The systematic analysis of UV absorption spectra provides valuable insights into the optical modifications induced by the incorporation of MFCD into the agarose‑sodium alginate matrix.

Again, the excitation-dependent emission features of the MAA film became apparent in the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of MFCDs coated MAA film which underwent analysis. The emission wavelength was seen to shift towards a higher wavelength with a drop in intensity when the excitation wavelength was increased [40]. Excitation of the film at 330, 340, 350, 360, 370, 380, 390, and 400 nm was monitored to generate fluorescence emissions at 398, 400, 403, 410, 425, 439, 448, and 454 nm, in the sequence shown in the Fig. 4(g).

The insertion of MFCDs exerts a discernible influence on the surface characteristics of the fabricated film, with consequences for hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity, as quantified by the contact angle (CA) measurement—a key indicator of surface wettability. The CA, representing the angle formed by a liquid drop on the substrate, serves as a crucial parameter in assessing surface interactions. When the CA exceeds 90°, the surface is classified as hydrophobic in relation to water, while a value below 90° indicates a hydrophilic character. The nanocomposite film (MAA) exhibited a reduced CA value of 52.1° compared to the CA value of bare film's i.e. 63.3° as shown in Fig. 4(h). This substantial reduction in the contact angle is indicative of an augmented hydrophilic nature resulting from the integration of the filler nanomaterial, MFCDs, onto the film surface. The enhanced hydrophilicity can be ascribed to the distinct interactions between the functional groups present in the CDs and the surrounding environment, leading to alterations in the film's surface energy and wetting behaviour. The detailed analysis of contact angle variations provides valuable insights into the interfacial properties and underscores the role of MFCDs in modulating the surface characteristics of the nanocomposite film.

Moreover, water absorbance capability of biopolymeric film is a crucial parameter in various applications. Based on the research, evaluating the water absorbance behaviour of the fabricated MAA film, the water intact capacity was found to reach a maximum of around 724 % at 5 min, remained almost constant upto 10 min and progressively decline over the next 20 min as shown in Fig. 4(i) and 4(j). Furthermore, the water solubility percentage of the MAA film was estimated to be ∼49.82 %. The excellent water absorbance and water solubility percentage reflects the good hydrophilicity of the MAA film. Also, the incorporated MFCDs might facilitate the water absorption capacity of MAA film.

The thermal behaviour of Na- alginate/agarose (Bare) film and MAA film is shown in Fig. 5(a). For MAA film, the degradation temperature starts at around 209 °C whereas degradation of bare film starts at around 227 °C. The initial degradation temperature of MAA film may be due to the early decomposition of the functional groups present in MFCD [43]. As a result, the initial degradation temperature of the MFCD coated MAA film was slightly higher (227 °C) than that of bare film. The DSC thermograms for the bare film and MAA film, are illustrated in Fig. 5(b). For the bare film, an endothermic peak was detected at ∼69 °C, indicating the loss of solvent, primarily due to the water evaporation process. Again, an exothermic peak was noted at about 237.8 °C which correspond to the degradation processes of the polymeric matrix. However, the MAA film exhibited an endothermic peak slightly higher at ∼67 °C within the same temperature range, also associated with moisture loss. This film showed exothermic peak at ∼229.9 °C, which is indicative of material decomposition or melting. The differences in thermal behaviour between two films can be attributed to the chemical changes occur after the functionalization of the biopolymeric film with MFCD, which offers valuable insight in potential applications.

In the investigation of MAA films for the optical detection of Fe3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions, varying concentrations of aqueous metal ion solutions were prepared. The metal ion solutions were meticulously prepared across a range of concentrations: 5 ppm, 1 ppm, 0.5 ppm, 0.1 ppm, 0.05 ppm, 0.01 ppm, and 0.005 ppm to evaluate the sensitivity of the prepared solid platform. The MAA films, cut into dimensions of 1 cm × 2 cm, underwent a 5-min immersion in each metal ion solution, allowing for a detailed examination of their fluorescence intensity responses.

Employing a high-precision fluorescence spectrophotometer, the optical quenching capability of the MAA film was assessed up to a concentration of 0.005 ppm for each metal and visually the optical quenching intensity was observed as shown in Fig. 6(a) under UV inspection cabinet. Notably, the MAA film's visual optical quenching performance for Hg2+, Pb2+, Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions were 0.1 ppm and up to 0.05 ppm concentrations, respectively whereas the visual quenching effect for Fe3+ ions remained remarkably responsive down to 0.005 ppm concentrations. The surface functional groups present in the carbon dot are responsible for coordinating with the metal ions which results in the fluorescence quenching. The functional groups promote selective interactions which transfer energy between MFCDs and the metal ions. Selectivity is affected by size, shape, edge structure, and surface functional groups of carbon dots. Furthermore, Different metal ions have unique electronic configurations, ionic radius and ionic charge which influence their reactivity and interaction with sensing moiety. Metals with smaller size and higher charges typically have stronger interactions potential leading to enhanced sensitivity. Moreover, the electrostatic interaction between negatively charged MFCDs moiety and positively charged different metal ions can influence the extent of sensitivity.

Upon close examination of the interaction dynamics between each heavy metal ion and the MAA film, a consistent decrease in fluorescence or photoluminescence intensity was observed providing the rising concentration of metal ions, particularly apparent at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. A Graphical representation in Fig. 6(b)-6(k) reveals the fluorescence intensity variations between untreated and metal-ion-treated MAA films with varying metal ion concentrations. The incremental rise in change in fluorescence or photoluminescence intensity (Δ intensity) with increasing metal ion concentrations (Fig. S7 in supporting information) can be utilized for the qualitative as well as quantitative assessment of heavy metal ions present in a water sample. The declining percentage of photoluminescence (PL) intensity of MAA film resulting from metal ion interaction shows an incremental order with elevated metal ion concentrations. Evaluating the variation in fluorescence intensity from Fig. 6, the interaction of MAA film with 0.005 ppm concentration of Fe3+ ions was found to be responsible for the highest decline in fluorescence intensity, approximately ∼17 %, among other metal ions.

Extending the analysis, a UV absorbance study was conducted to delve into the spectral changes induced by the interaction of heavy metal ions with MAA film. The results highlighted a slight systematic increase in absorbance with elevated concentrations of heavy metal ions interacting to the biopolymeric film. This trend signifies that an elevated quantity of metal ions in the sample solution corresponds to a heightened level of interaction with the carbon dots, consequently amplifying the quenching effect. The graphical representation in UV absorption, focused around the 270 nm wavelength range, provided additional insights into the wavelength shifts induced by metal ion interactions. Fig. 7 reveals a slight progressive rise in Δ Absorbance as metal ion concentrations increase. This Δ Absorbance is the estimated absorbance difference between the untreated MAA film and that of heavy metal ions treated.

In the comprehensive exploration of MFCDs coated biopolymeric (MAA) film for the optical detection of Fe3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ ions, an in-depth analysis revealed remarkable phenomena in the fluorescence behaviour of the MAA film upon exposure to heavy metal ion solutions.

When it comes to both theoretical and applied research, fluorescence represents as one of the most remarkable properties of CDs. Owing to the intricate structures and functionalities that produce a variety of optical impacts, CDs' probable fluorescence mechanisms can be described either by the presence of surface chemical groups or the quantum confinement impact for the bandgap energy shifts within the conjugated π-system. CDs' ability to undergo surface oxidation is one of the reasons for their fluorescence. An elevated degree of surface oxidation is responsible for red-shifted emission as the bandgap contracts due to the increase of oxygen concentrations on the surface of CD. As functional groups like CO and CN are evenly distributed on the CD surface, their ability to supply electrons is likewise increased, as is the energy they generate. Also, the fluorescence is triggered by the quantum confinement effect of the conjugated π-electrons present in the CDs moiety. The valence and conduction band transformation are covered in the quantum confinement effect phenomena where continuous energy bands evolved into discrete energy states as the materials' size drops to the nanoscale level, and as a consequence of this the bandgap rises [44].

Numerous mechanisms of CDs quenching are known till date upon heavy metal ion quenching. CDs embedded sensing platforms may rely on the static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and inner filter effect (IFE). To assess the probable mechanism of fluorescence quenching in this study, we have used the Stern-Volmer theory, which distinguishes between two forms of photoluminescence quenching viz. static and dynamic. During the photon excitation process, the quencher molecules present within an interaction sphere surrounding the luminescent species (such as CDs), positioned at an appropriate distance to form a non-fluorescent complex, which leads to immediate quenching of the luminescence property. Whereas, if the photoluminescence quenching occurs due to the collision between the luminescent species and the quencher molecule, it is referred as dynamic or collisional quenching [45]. The evaluation of Stern- Volmer plot, represented as F
0
/F vs. [Q], where F
0 denotes the photoluminescence intensity without the presence of quencher, F represents the photoluminescence intensity following interaction with the quencher, and [Q] signifies the concentration of the quencher heavy metal ions significantly. This analysis was conducted to understand the fluorescence quenching response of the MAA film upon interaction with heavy metal ions. The representation of Stern-Volmer equation in quenching mechanism, is shown in Eq. (3) [46], where K
SV is the Stern- Volmer constant:
(3)

F

K SV

If both static and dynamic quenching involves in the quenching phenomenon then the followed Stern-Volmer plot takes the form as Eq. (4), where, K
S and K D are Stern-Volmer constants for static and dynamic quenching correspondingly:
(4)

1 +

By plotting F
0
/F against [Q], we observed a downward polynomial curvature (Fig. 8(a), (b), (c), (d) and (e)) towards x-axis for Stern-Volmer plot unlike a linear one, which likely indicates probably photoluminescence quenching occurring only through a fraction of the fluorophores (f
1 ), while the remaining were inaccessible for quenching and probably independent of the quencher concentration [46,47]. The Stern-Volmer equation would take the form outlined in Eq. (5) attributable to the dependency of fluorescence intensity (F) on the quencher concentration [46]:
(5)

For each quenching ion, we estimated the R2 value of the polynomial curve and the Stern- Volmer constant (K
SV ) in order to Figure out the probable quenching behaviour. The higher the R2 value of a polynomial graph, highlighting the accuracy towards following single quenching mechanism. For all of the quencher ions in this particular study, K
SV along with R2 values have been displayed in Table 2 .

Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime decay of MAA film was examined with or without the interaction of heavy metal ions (Fig. 8(f)). Fluorescence lifetime is also a measure that refers to the decrease in the duration of fluorescence emission from a fluorophore molecule due to the interaction with quencher molecule. The average fluorescence lifetime of MAA film was determined to be 1.63 ns whereas the values differ after being treated with heavy metal ions. These values for Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ and Cd2+ ions when interacted with MAA film were 0.89 ns, 0.42 ns, 0.62 ns, 1.25 ns and 1.21 ns accordingly. The graphical representations of fluorescence lifetime decay measurement have been presented in the Supporting information (Fig. S8). This points out the sensing mechanism is probably based on dynamic quenching [48], signifying that the electron transferred from the fluorophore molecule in its excited state and reducing the fluorescence lifetime [49].

The biopolymers, sodium-alginate and agarose, embedded in MAA film have explicit binding ability with heavy metal ions through coordinate linkage as they have hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in its structural moiety. The absorption of heavy metal ions in the alginate-agarose composite film may lead to the slight increase in the absorbance intensity with elevating metal ion concentrations as observed in UV–Vis spectroscopy study. Even if the biopolymers' propensity to bind to heavy metal ions may result in static quenching, the formation of a non-fluorescent complex in the ground state is ruled out based on the downward polynomial Stern-Volmer plot [50,51]. However, for the ground state complexation, a little weak interaction was expected to emerge.

Assessing the experiment's specificity was identified on the distinctive chemical groups residing on the surface of MFCDs, showcasing explicit interaction capabilities with the selective metal ions. Within the dynamic quenching mechanism, the fluorescence of CDs underwent quenching as the formed complex transitioned back to the ground state without emitting photons [49]. The mechanism underscoring the intricate relationship between the physical properties of CDs and their behaviour in the presence of specific metal ions. This quenching phenomenon was predominantly ascribed to functional groups, including -COOH, -OH, -C=O, and -C=N, as referenced in relevant literature [52].

Moreover, based on the concept of ion exchange, another possible mechanism could potentially be explained. The Na+ ions present in the MAA film would release into the heavy metal ion solution and eventually exchange with the metal cations, thereby, an electrostatic interaction exhibits between the metal cations and negatively charged surface functional groups of the fluorophore molecule (MFCD) [53].

The study further addressed into the specific interaction mechanism with Fe3+ ions, where an intriguing electron transfer took place between MFCD and vacant d-orbitals of Fe3+. As a result of the trivalent iron ions' higher positive charges than those of other divalent heavy metal cations, they are more likely to interact with the surface groups like -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 of MFCD incorporated on MAA film.

The electron transfer mechanism, in case of Fe3+ as well as Cu2+, Cd2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ sheds light on the precision of dynamic or collisional quenching more than that of static quenching, contributing to the understanding of the optical detection mechanism in the realm of heavy metal ions (Fig. 9 ).

A comparative assessment of the limit of detection (LOD) of various metal ions is presented in Table 3 , highlights the effectiveness of present work on Mesua Ferrea derived novel carbon dot incorporated MAA film. Our study found to be reliable and substantially suitable for visual optical detection of metal ions like Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+ Cd2+ and Pb2+. However, to enhance the utility of MAA film, its stability should be studied under different environmental conditions. The mechanical strength of MAA film may pose certain limitations for other practical implications. Furthermore, the concentration of the nanomaterial utilized can impact the sensitivity and effectiveness of MAA film which may lead to inconsistent performance in detection of specific heavy metals. Moreover, challenges related to scalability in fabrication techniques must be addressed for large-scale applications and commercialization, as variations in fabrication techniques and environmental factors can influence film properties. Though MAA film is an eco-friendly substitute of other conventional sensing methods yet their long-term preservation is quite difficult due to microbial degradation which may be controlled by proper treatment.

In light of the widespread environmental pollution, the present research efficiently responses the growing need for effective and environmentally benign detection methods for heavy metal ion. The innovative approach of utilizing Mesua Ferrea Carbon Dots (MFCDs) coated biopolymeric films, comprising Na-alginate and agarose, demonstrates promising potential for optical detection applications. The hydrothermal synthesis of MFCD, resulting in nano-sized particles below 10 nm with distinctive photoluminescence characteristics enhances the appeal of this approach. The successful incorporation of MFCD into the biopolymeric (MAA) film, contributing to the film's optical sensitivity. The optical study revealing the quenching effect of MAA films in response to varying concentrations of heavy metal ions, systematically analysed through fluorescence intensity and UV absorbance studies, underscores the specificity and sensitivity of the developed sensor platform. Entirely, this work delivers an optically active solid platform for the development of heavy metal ion sensors that are simultaneously sustainable and economically viable, thereby facilitating efficient environmental monitoring and sensing technologies.
